title: When will AI-related job losses stop being bullish for stocks?

bad that the aggregate credit card and
and auto loan delinquency rates are
essentially at GFC-type levels.
I mean, think about how bad it must be
for the folks on the bottom of the K for
the aggregate delinquency rates, which
include people like us, to be at, you
know, GFC-type levels.
And so, you know, that means if you're
on the bottom of the K, your delinquency
rate's all the way up here. You know,
you're all the way up here. You're
making new highs. And so, you know, this
is the point I'm making is that the
stock market doesn't care about this.
The stock market doesn't care about the
people on the bottom of the K. And you
know who's about to join them?
Disrupted and displaced white-collar
workers, the folks who are working at
Meta, the folks who are working at
Microsoft that no longer have jobs.
Stock market's not going to care about
them, too. And so, again, I'm not being
I'm I'm
I'm This sounds callous. I'm not trying
to be callous. I care about these people
as a human as a human, but the reality
is the stock market doesn't care about
these people. The stock market is
emotionless. Stock market is a is a
corporate beast.
So, you know, let's just you have to
disassociate your, you know, whatever
your political views or your societal
views or your spiritual views, if your
goal was to stay on the right side of
market risk, you have to leave all that
stuff at the door, and that's what we're
doing here. Uh the strong positive
impulse
in March core retail sales to 7.4%
through with annualized
supports our nearly four-year-old view
that historically positive household
sector balance sheet dynamics are
allowing the consumption of the allowing
the growth of consumption among
households at the top of the K-shaped US
economy to outpace the decline in real
purchasing power among households at the
bottom of the K, which is a core tenant
of our West Village Montauk uh thesis.
Uh review our our glossary if you want
to get more details on that. Uh AI could
perpetuate this dynamic if productivity
growth allows the growth in labor income
among the top AI adopters to outpace the
decline in labor income from displaced
workers. So, essentially what we're
saying is
as long as the people who are adopting
the AI and still have jobs, as long as
their income growth is faster than the
displaced income, the the the the lost
income from the displaced workers, then
you can have a dynamic that could
sustain consumption.
But, you know, that's no guarantees, but
it I mean, we've already seen this
occur, you know, in the in the in the in
the We've already seen this occur in the
in terms of the our resilient US economy
theme, which is essentially saying rich
people matter more
to the the the the dynamic the the the
growth the rate of change of the growth
cycle in the economy. Uh and we've seen
this for years since since we adopted
that theme, and we would expect that to
continue uh in terms of this AI dynamic
and its impact on the on the labor
market. Eventually, it's going to
eventually going to displace too many
workers. But, the point I'm making from
here is that the starting from this
starting point, the next trade is now
this stuff booms productivity and booms
profits. The trade after that might be,
okay, we've displaced too many workers,
now what? What what are the political
ramifications? What are the regulatory
ramifications? What are the societal
ramifications? Cough cough, civil
[clears throat] war. Uh We'll keep
moving here. Uh from a profitability
standpoint,
corporations win in the following two
scenarios. Number one, if our
productivity if our paradigm C theme and
our productivity boom theme and our jobs
recovery theme combine to create a
scenario where gross domestic income
growth is outpacing the growth of
nominal employee compensation, profits
go up in that scenario. They also go up
in the scenario where a jobless recovery
basically means that we're we're losing
so many jobs and we're not sustaining
consumption and GDP growth that you have
a decline in gross domestic income.
However, if the decline in gross
domestic income is less than the decline
in nominal employee compensation, you
could still actually grow profits.
You know, profits is a residual
statistic. It's not an aggregate
statistic. It's a residual of the rate
of change of sales and the rate of
change of margins. And so, you're
essentially saying is if AI creates more
margins, you know, you can actually have
an economy with that that sort of
deflates. But, as long as the the the
the the the the deflation in the
employee compensation is faster than the
deflation in the revenue part of the
equation, then you're going to have more
profits. And so, I I think the
corporations are well aware of this, and
you know, we we talked about this months
ago, if not quarters several quarters
ago. We said the promise of AI is that
the blue line falls off the bottom of
the page, and the red line jumps off the
top of the page. Well, guess what? I
updated the chart today, and the blue
line made an all-time low. Red line made
an all-time high. The red line being
corporate profits as a share of gross
domestic income, the blue line being
nominal employee compensation as a share
of gross domestic income. So, the blue
line is falling off the bottom of the
page as we predicted. Red line is
jumping off the top of the page as we
predicted. And guess what the stock
market is.
So, this is the promise of AI. So, this
is what we've been saying for for months
and quarters, and ultimately it's not
going to be great for society, but, you
know, our job is not to fix society. Our
job is to stay on the right side of
market risk. So, let's keep doing that.
Uh with 137
of 499 companies having reported Q1
results thus far, uh 27% S&P 500
constituent revenue growth has
accelerated to nearly 10%
year-over-year, which is the highest
since 3Q22 2022 in absolute terms, or
the highest since 2Q2022 in
delta-adjusted terms and rate of change
terms, which is nearly double the
long-run mean of 5%. The beat rate
accelerated to 68%, which is well above
its long-run mean of 55%. So, I mean,
just sales are booming. Uh S&P 500
constituent earnings growth is unchanged
quarter-over-quarter at 13.7%
year-over-year, which is comfortably
above its long-run mean of 9.5%. The
beat rate accelerated to 80%, which is
comfortably above its long-run mean of
75%.
Consensus 2026 sales and earnings growth
expectations for S&P 500 constituents
are violently accelerating to fresh
cycle highs of 9.6% and 18.5%
respectively. This is pulling forward
some of the growth expected in 2027 uh
for sales and earnings, uh but the
expectation of 7% sales growth and 16.2%
earnings per share growth are well above
their respective long-run means of 5%
and uh 9.5% respectively. So, even
though we're pulling forward uh earnings
our sales and earnings growth into 2026,
it the the the the the the the growth
that we're going to see that consensus
is expecting for 2027 is still
comfortably above uh the long-run mean.
So, there's already a productivity boom
sort of implied in the in the in the in
the consensus expectations, and those
expectations are trending higher at
least for 2026 uh sales and earnings. Uh
the median one-day stock price reaction
to a revenue beat is outpacing the
reaction to a revenue miss, uh and which
implies the buy side is hopeful and keen
to reward sales beats. The median
one-day stock price reaction to an
earnings per share beat is lagging uh
the reaction to an earnings per share
miss, which implies the buy side is in
show-me mode and keen to punish earnings
misses. So, this this revenue dynamic is
something that's, you know, that's
really it's really positive. That's what
the market wants. It wants sales growth,
uh and it's really rewarding uh that
sales growth.
